content: The length of three sides of a trapezium are equal, each being 10 cms . Then, the maximum area $\left(\mathrm{cm}^2\right)$ of the trapezium is
explanation:
Let, $A P=x \mathrm{~cm}$

$$\because \triangle A P D \sim \triangle B Q C$$

So, $B Q=x \mathrm{~cm}$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Now, Area }(A)=\frac{1}{2}(10+(10+2 x)) \times \text { height } \\ & \text { In } \triangle A P D, D P=h=\sqrt{10^2-x^2}=\sqrt{100-x^2} \\ & \therefore \quad A(x)=\frac{1}{2}(2(10+x))\left(\sqrt{100-x^2}\right) \end{aligned}$$

$=(10+x)\sqrt{100-x^2}$

For maximum area, find $A^{\prime}(x)$

$$\begin{aligned} A^{\prime}(x) & =(10+x)\left(\frac{-2 x}{2 \sqrt{100-x^2}}\right)+\left(\sqrt{100-x^2}\right) \\ & =\frac{-x(10+x)+100-x^2}{\sqrt{100-x^2}} \\ & =\frac{-2 x^2-10 x+100}{\sqrt{100-x^2}} \end{aligned}$$

Now, $A^{\prime}(x)=0$ gives

$$2 x^2+10 x-100=0 \Rightarrow x=5 \text { and }-10$$

$\because x \neq-10$ (Negative)

$\therefore$ Area will be maximum at $x=5$

$$\begin{aligned} \text{So, }\quad A(5) & =(10+5) \sqrt{100-25}=15 \sqrt{75} \\ & =75 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \end{aligned}$$

content: Difference between the maximum and minimum values of $f(x)=-\sin ^3 x+3 \sin ^2 x+5$ in $x \in\left[0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]$ is
explanation:
To find the difference between the maximum and minimum values of $ f(x) = -\sin^3 x + 3 \sin^2 x + 5 $ for $ x \in \left[0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right] $, we first compute the derivative:

$ \begin{aligned} f^{\prime}(x) &= -3 \sin^2 x \cos x + 6 \sin x \cos x \\ &= -3 \sin x \cos x (\sin x - 2). \end{aligned} $

This derivative $ f^{\prime}(x) $ indicates that $ f^{\prime}(x) \geq 0 $ over the interval, which means $ f(x) $ is strictly increasing on $\left[0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]$.

Therefore, the minimum value of $ f(x) $ occurs at the start of the interval:

$ f(x)_{\text{min}} = f(0) = 5. $

And the maximum value occurs at the end of the interval:

$ f(x)_{\text{max}} = f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right) = -1 + 3(1) + 5 = 7. $

Hence, the difference between the maximum and minimum values is:

$ 7 - 5 = 2. $

content: If tangent to the curve $f(x)=x^3-\alpha x^2-x+\beta$ at point $(1,3)$ on the curve, cut equals non zero intercepts on co-ordinate axes, then
explanation:
Given that the curve is defined by $ f(x) = x^3 - \alpha x^2 - x + \beta $, and that the tangent at point $ (1,3) $ intersects the axes at equal non-zero intercepts, we proceed as follows:

Verify the Point on the Curve:

Since $ (1, 3) $ lies on the curve, we substitute $ x = 1 $ into the curve equation:

$ f(1) = 1^3 - \alpha \cdot 1^2 - 1 + \beta = 3 $

Simplifying, we get:

$ 1 - \alpha - 1 + \beta = 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \beta - \alpha = 3 $

Determine the Slope of the Tangent:

The tangent cuts equal intercepts; hence, its slope must be $-1$.

Find the Derivative and Set the Slope:

To find the slope at $ x = 1 $, calculate the derivative $ f'(x) $:

$ f'(x) = 3x^2 - 2\alpha x - 1 $

At $ x = 1 $:

$ f'(1) = 3(1)^2 - 2\alpha(1) - 1 = -1 $

This simplifies to:

$ 3 - 2\alpha - 1 = -1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2 - 2\alpha = -1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad -2\alpha = -3 $

Solving for $\alpha$:

$ 2\alpha = 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = \frac{3}{2} $

Calculate $\beta$:

Using $\beta - \alpha = 3$, substitute $\alpha = \frac{3}{2}$:

$ \beta - \frac{3}{2} = 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \beta = 3 + \frac{3}{2} = \frac{9}{2} $

Sum $\alpha + \beta$:

$ \alpha + \beta = \frac{3}{2} + \frac{9}{2} = \frac{12}{2} = 6 $

Therefore, the sum $\alpha + \beta = 6$.

content: The maximum slope of the curve $$y=\frac{1}{2} x^4-5 x^3+18 x^2-19 x+7$$ occurs at the point
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { } \because y=\frac{1}{2} x^4-5 x^3+18 x^2-19 x+7 \quad \text{.... (i)}\\ & \therefore \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=2 x^3-15 x^2+36 x-19 \quad \text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

Again differentiating w.r.t. $$x$$,

$$\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=6 x^2-30 x+36\quad \text{.... (iii)}$$

When, $$\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=0 \Rightarrow 6\left(x^2-5 x+6\right)=0$$

$$\Rightarrow x^2-3 x-2 x+6=0 \Rightarrow x(x-3)-2(x-3)=0$$

So, $$x=3$$ and $$x=2$$

Differentiating Eq. (iii) w.r.t. $$x, \frac{d^3 y}{d x^3}=12 x-30$$

Now, at $$x=2, \frac{d^3 y}{d x^3}=-6<0$$

At $$\because \quad x=3, \frac{d^3 y}{d x^3}=+6>0$$

$$\therefore x=2$$ is point of maximum

$$\therefore$$ At $$x=2$$ slope will be maximum.

So, from Eq. (i)

$$y=\frac{1}{2}(2)^4-5(2)^3+18(2)^2-19(2)+7=9$$

$$\therefore$$ Maximum slope occurs at $$(2,9)$$

content: $$f$$ and $$g$$ are differentiable function in $$(0,1)$$ satisfying $$f(0)=2=g(\mathrm{l}), g(0)=0$$ and $$f(l)=6$$, then for some $$c \in] 0,1[$$
explanation:
Here, $$f(0)=2=g(1), g(0)=0$$ and $$f(1)=6$$

$$\because f$$ and $$g$$ are differentiable in $$(1,0)$$.

Let $$h(x)=f(x)-2 g(x)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & h(0)=f(0)-2 g(0) \\ & h(0)=2-0=2 \end{aligned}$$

Now, $$h(1)=f(1)-2 g(1)=6-2(2)$$

$$h(1)=2, h(0)=h(1)=2$$

Hence, using Rolle's theorem, there exist $$C \in] 0,1[$$ such that

$$\begin{array}{ll} & h^{\prime}(c)=0 \\ \Rightarrow & f^{\prime}(c)=2 g^{\prime}(c)=0, \text { for some } \\ & C \in] 0,1[ \\ \Rightarrow \quad & f^{\prime}(c)=2 g^{\prime}(c) \end{array}$$

content: The maximum slope of the curve $$y=\frac{1}{2} x^4-5 x^3+18 x^2-19 x$$ occurs at the point
explanation:
Given curve is

$$y=\frac{1}{2} x^4-5 x^3+18 x^2-19 x \quad \text{.... (i)}$$

Slope of the curve $$=\frac{d y}{d x}$$

Differentiating Eq. (i) w.r.t. $$x$$,

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2}\left(4 x^3\right)-5\left(3 x^2\right)+18(2 x)-19 \\ & \frac{d y}{d x}=2 x^3-15 x^2+36 x-19=f(x) \text { (say) } \quad \text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

Again differentiating w.r.t. $$x$$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} f^{\prime}(x) & =2\left(3 x^2\right)-15(2 x)+36 \\ & =6 x^2-30 x+36 \quad \text{.... (iii)} \end{aligned}$$

Equating $$f^{\prime}(x)=0$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & 6 x^2-30 x+36=0 \\ \Rightarrow & x^2-5 x+6=0 \\ \Rightarrow & x^2-3 x-2 x+6=0 \\ \Rightarrow & x(x-3)-2(x-3)=0 \\ \Rightarrow & (x-3)(x-2)=0 \\ & x=3 \text { and } x=2 \end{array}$$

Differentiating Eq. (iii) w.r.t. $$x$$, we get $$f^{\prime \prime}(x)=12 x-30$$

Now, $$f^{\prime \prime}(2)=12(2)-30=24-30=-6<0$$

and $$f^{\prime \prime}(3)=12(3)-30=36-30=6>0$$

Since $$f^{\prime \prime}(2)<0 \Rightarrow 2$$ is point of maximum

$$\therefore$$ At $$x=2$$, slope will be maximum.

$$\therefore$$ From Eq. (i)

$$\begin{aligned} y= & \frac{1}{2}(2)^4-5(2)^3+18(2)^2-19(2) \\ \quad & \quad \text { substituting } x=2 \text { in Eq. (i)] } \\ y= & 8-40+72-38 \\ = & 80-78=2 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ Maximum slope occurs at point $$(2,2)$$

content: The interval in which the function $$f(x)=\sin x-\cos x, 0 \leq x \leq 2 \pi$$ is strictly decreasing, is
explanation:
Given function is, $$f(x)=\sin x-\cos x$$, where $$x \in[0,2 \pi]$$

Now, $$f^{\prime}(x)=\cos x+\sin x$$.

For strictly decreasing of $$f(x)$$; put $$f^{\prime}(x)<0$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad \sin x+\cos x<0$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sin x+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cos x< 0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad\left(\sin x \cos \frac{\pi}{4}+\cos x \sin \frac{\pi}{4}\right)< 0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)< 0 \\ & \because \quad \sin x< 0 \text { when } \pi < x < 2 \pi \\ & \therefore \quad \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)<0 \text { when } \pi< x+\frac{\pi}{4}<2 \pi \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \pi-\frac{\pi}{4}< x <2 \pi-\frac{\pi}{4} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{3 \pi}{4}< x <\frac{7 \pi}{4} \end{aligned}$$

content: The slope of normal to the curve $$y=x^3+2 x+6$$ which is parallel to line $$x+14 y+4=0$$ is
explanation:
Given curve is

$$y=x^3+2 x+6$$

On differentiation w.r.t. $$x$$, we get

$$\frac{d y}{d x}=3 x^2+2$$

Now, slope of normal $$=\frac{-1}{(d y / d x)}=\frac{-1}{3 x^2+2}$$

Since, the normal is parallel to the line

$$\begin{array}{cc} & x+14 y+4=0 \\ \Rightarrow & -\frac{1}{14}=-\frac{1}{3 x^2+2}\left[m_1=m_2\right] \\ \Rightarrow & 3 x^2+2=14 \\ \Rightarrow & 3 x^2=12 \Rightarrow \quad x^2=4 \\ \therefore & x= \pm 2 \end{array}$$

$$\therefore$$ Required slope of normal $$=\frac{-1}{3(4)+2}=\frac{-1}{14}$$
